item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the last batch of tests, several O5 try to undergo transcendence by making Transcendence become Charles, then Injecting themselves into it. That said, -1, -2 and -3 become inside rather than go inside. From that, I believe that when Inject failed due to -1, -2 and -3 being wholly unable to "transcend" for whatever reason, Point was cast instead. Therefore, here are my hypotheses:
If the target of Inject is an abstract concept while Charles is a physical concept, target is outside.
If the target of Inject cannot conform in any conceptual or physical way to being "inside" Charles, Point is cast on the target instead.

My last hypothesis was initially "If Point is cast on Charles, Charles is outside", but while it would conform to the logs, it would make no logical sense. Following previous nomenclature, casting Point on Charles should rather return "Is inside" or "Was inside".
Therefore, here is my last hypothesis :
Some concepts, such as "SCP-682" and "Transcendence", are currently outside and cannot become inside.

item.title: upvoted long ago but still gotta make this joke-
item.content:encoded:
Rating Became inside
+1 Went inside

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"any more explanation destroys the game being played"

Well there are some games that are just not good to play. This SCP is trying to be "you can't even understand what this is! isn't that cool!" but it comes across more like a "lolololol gibberish lololol" shitpost meme, like the page image for this 1d4chan article: https://1d4chan.org/wiki/What.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's like that logical fallacy they teach you in philosophy where someone goes

"Stevie Wonder is blind.

Love is blind

Therefore, Stevie Wonder is love"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

…

…

…

[gets up from desk]

That's it, I'm done for the day.

[shut's door]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nailed it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took me a While to figure this out, but I really like it now. I feel like the really unsettling element once you have it worked out is the fact that O5-4 managed to get "inside" the concept of Transcendence and after that it seems to stop working…

(also, I wonder if the reason intestinal distress ended up "outside" for bright is because it assumed "doctor bright" to be the amulet, as opposed to the current body?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay! I figured it out!

Pay attention to the differences between "became inside" and "went inside". Things marked as "went inside" go inside/become applied to the latest thing to "became inside". For example:

O5 - became inside
Dr. Zemelo - went inside
Punishment - became inside
O5-7 - went inside

This means that Dr Zemelo used this object to make himself an O5, and the O5 council then used the object to punish him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks finally got it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know your reply is mostly farcical, but in the interest of maintaining clarity on what I hope is a very thorough explanation of this thing's effects, I have to contradict you.

Remember "outside" designates a failure of 2719, but it doesn't say whether it was a failure of Point or Inject. The 682 saga here ends with three instances of "went inside" followed by one "outside". IMO, it makes more sense if that "outside" represents another attempted use of Inject, which failed because 682 repeatedly put itself inside of itself until 2719 broke. Later, we have "Dr. Bright: became inside" followed directly by "intestinal distress: outside (nice try)". This "outside" also makes more sense to me as a failed attempt at Inject, because that would say that someone attempted to give Dr. Bright intestinal distress. As to why it failed, I'm not totally clear; my best guess is that the entire concept of intestinal distress cannot be contained within a person, so perhaps "an instance of intestinal distress" would have given the desired outcome.

Regardless, it seems most likely to me that those two examples from the table are failed attempts to use the Inject part of 2719, which contradicts the hypothesis that Inject alters objects or reality to make its target go inside Charles. I can't see any examples from the table that do support that hypothesis, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like if the inject function would not normally work, it alters objects or reality to match; car is Charles, inject democracy, car has steering wheels on all seats, car turns in majority direction.

Other government types:
Monarchy: car unchanged.
Oligarchy: see democracy, but limited to front seats.
Timocracy: car becomes carrier truck or maybe tank.
Anarchy: car becomes several mopeds.
Technocracy: car is self-driving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh God, it all makes sense now. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, I like this one a lot, so I'll give it a go.

+ Long explanation ahoy
- Wow I wrote too much

2719 isn't really an object; it'd be easier to think of it like a spell you can cast. Actually, two spells you can cast, but they're related to each other. For this explanation, let's say one spell is called "Point" and the other is called "Inject".

The Point spell doesn't have any easily-observable effect, it just magically points to the thing you cast it on. It keeps pointing to that thing until you cast it on something else. In the article, the thing that the Point spell currently points to has a secret, specific, unique title. In the article, the title is "inside", but that's only so that the article can intentionally confuse you. I don't want to confuse you, so for this explanation I'm going to pretend the title is "Charles" instead. The title is arbitrary, so it doesn't matter if I change it. So, when you cast Point on something, that thing becomes Charles. On the table in the article, this is listed as "became inside".

The Inject spell puts whatever you cast it on inside of Charles. On the table in the article, this is listed as "went inside". Unlike with "became inside", the word "inside" here is being used in accordance with that word's commonly-understood dictionary definition; it isn't an arbitrary title that I can replace with another word. Inject literally puts its target inside of whatever is currently Charles.

The only reason the title "Charles" is relevant to anything is because the Inject spell needs to know where to put its target. The target of the Point spell doesn't observably change when it becomes Charles. It doesn't lose any properties or other names that it had, it doesn't know that it now has the title of Charles, people don't inexplicably understand that it's Charles. If I cast Point on my cat, it would still behave in every way like an orange, fluffy, exceptionally stupid cat named Pounce. Just, in addition to "orange", "fluffy", "exceptionally stupid", "cat", and "Pounce", it is now secretly also "Charles." The current Charles stops being Charles when I cast Point on something else. So later, if I cast Point on the shed in my backyard, my shed is Charles and my cat isn't. The cat is still a normal cat, the shed is still a normal shed.

If my shed is Charles and I cast Inject on my lawnmower, my lawnmower would physically relocate to the inside of my shed, because Inject puts its target inside of Charles and my shed is Charles. If I then cast Inject on my ladder, my ladder would also physically relocate to the inside of my shed; the shed didn't stop being Charles just because I cast Inject once already. The lawnmower and the ladder would both be inside of my shed, just as if I had manually carried them in there myself.

That's the gist of it. Point makes its target Charles. Inject puts its target inside of whatever is currently Charles. It doesn't matter what Charles was before, or what Charles will be later. These spells are instantaneous and don't have an ongoing or retroactive effect. You cast them, they do their thing, and then they're done. So, following along with the above example, I'm free to take the ladder out of my shed and leave it in my yard, even though my shed is still Charles and I previously put the ladder inside of the shed with Inject. If I then cast Point on my garage, nothing changes except that my garage is now Charles and my shed isn't. The lawnmower stays in the shed, the ladder stays in the yard. If I cast Inject on my car, it goes inside the garage, not the shed.

The targets of these spells don't have to be things you can currently see when you cast them. They don't even have to be physical objects. SCP-2719 works on anything you can name, even concepts and locations.

The Inject spell tries to make the most sensible, non-destructive attempt that it can to put the thing it targets inside of Charles, within the semantic limits of the word "inside". If Inject's target can sensibly be put somewhere inside Charles, it puts it there (car is Charles, Inject person, person inside car cabin, not inside gas tank). If Inject's target doesn't fit inside of Charles physically, it shoves it in anyway (person is Charles, Inject car, car can't physically fit inside of person, person explodes). If Inject's target can't be meaningfully put inside of Charles in any way, Inject fails (car is Charles, Inject democracy, Inject fails). In the rare case that Inject's target doesn't physically fit inside Charles, but might conceptually be put inside of Charles for some definition of "inside", it does that (person A is Charles, Inject person B, person B inside person A, both A and B are consenting adults).

The Point spell can also fail to call something Charles, if that thing is not within SCP-2719's scope. Some things are too powerful or too dangerous to be affected by SCP-2719.

Even given that SCP-2719 has some sort of finite scope, you can see how this could easily be abused (Point "my warehouse", Inject "all gold in Ft. Knox"). We can intuit from the first paragraph that any sapient, biological creature with enough information about SCP-2719 can use it, so our assumption about it being some sort of spell or ritual or something probably isn't too far off. It seems from the experiment logs that it's not intuitively obvious to people interacting with SCP-2719 what either of the spells do, nor is it easy to figure out whether you're casting Point or Inject without training. The table in the article doesn't tell you which of the two spells was attempted, but it does tell you the result. If the result was "became inside", they successfully cast the Point spell; read it as "became Charles". If the result was "went inside", they successfully cast the Inject spell; read it as "went inside of Charles". If the result was "outside", you don't explicitly know which spell they tried to cast, but it failed (and you can usually make an educated guess).

Armed with that understanding, the table in the article tells a story. Other people have summarized that story here already. Some of it is a little ambiguous, but hopefully you should be able to figure out the gist of it on your own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this SCP a couple months ago while going through 682's termination logs - I honestly didn't understand it at the time.

Now I read it again and went through every single comment - still no idea what this is about. I'd love to understand it, but I would need a laymen explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's pretty damn comprehensible even without a coding background, provided your mind works a certain way. Still a bit bothered by (or perhaps just having a bit of trouble understanding?) the logs where the result is "Outside", since the description mandates that the thing indicated either become or go inside, with no possibility of failure.
All the things people are saying detract from it (682 crosstest, lolFoundation, etc.) imo serve to make it "friendlier" to the site while also helping the reader make sense of what the skip does.

+1nside

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok this is kinda cool, but it looked like total gibberish to me until I saw that code discussion.
The use of 'inside' as both the noun and adjective in the description is what I think throws many people off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hi

no

best regards,
the author

item.title: Re: Did I miss it in eight pages, or has no one done this yet?
item.content:encoded:
The scip itself is the pointer, no need for a list of pointers in a class.

In C pointer notation, with commentary:

+ show block
– hide block
 // variable declaration and initial state concept *scp_2719 = &opel_astra_2008; // "Went inside" means something was assigned to (i.e. placed inside of) SCP-2719 // "Became inside" means that thing is now what the pointer is pointing at, i.e. you take the "conceptual address" of it // "Outside" presumably means the assignment failed, for whatever reason *scp_2719 = d_5789; *scp_2719 = d_5794; // the two D-class enter the car scp_2719 = &d_5796; // first successful reassignment *scp_2719 = d_5802; // the D-class "goes inside" the other one? somehow D-5796 survives *scp_2719 = containment_unit_a; // the D-class is definitely dead after this *scp_2719 = containment_unit_b; // there are now two containment chambers inside this D-class scp_2719 = &containment_unit_c; *scp_2719 = d_5803; *scp_2719 = d_5805; *scp_2719 = d_5812; // still doing initial testing, they're having D-class enter the chamber scp_2719 = &d_5813; // accidentally assigns SCP-2719 to the D-class instead of the other way around scp_2719 = &containment_unit_b; // they fix the assignment scp_2719 = &oulu_finland; *scp_2719 = population_of_oulu; // by "moving" all of Oulu into the city, they successfully evacuate it. // no idea what this looks like on a massive scale, does everybody just // walk into the city without realizing what's happening? scp_2719 = &containment_unit_682; // since SCP-682 is a metaphysical entity, they're going to do some termination cross-testing *scp_2719 = scp_682; // very convenient for moving a dangerous anomaly easily scp_2719 = &scp_682; *scp_2719 = scp_682; *scp_2719 = scp_682; *scp_2719 = scp_682; // they move SCP-682 inside itself? probably very destructive, or maybe a new containment system *scp_2719 = scp_682; // FAILS: nothing works forever on SCP-682, it eventually escapes scp_2719 = &o5; // now we're playing with more amorphous things, like the concept of being an Overseer *scp_2719 = dr_zermelo; // this cheeky doctor tries to promote themselves to O5 // (note that this is equivalent to manually specifying o5_7 = dr_zermelo) scp_2719 = &punishment; *scp_2719 = o5_7; // ...and they get punished for it scp_2719 = &dr_bright; *scp_2719 = intestinal_distress; // FAILS: somebody tries to give Bright a stomachache? kind of lolfoundation scp_2719 = &intestinal_distress; *scp_2719 = dr_blackbox; // Bright gets his revenge // But he makes the doctor, presumably a human, go _inside_ the concept of stomach illness. // How does that even work?? scp_2719 = &taree_australia; *scp_2719 = population_of_taree; // another evacuation. to be honest, this could've been excluded from the article scp_2719 = &transcendence; // The Overseers start playing with even higher concepts: they try to make themselves // "transcend", whatever that means. However some assignments are apparently more difficult // than others, since they seem to be having a bit of trouble scp_2719 = &o5_1; scp_2719 = &transcendence; scp_2719 = &o5_2; scp_2719 = &transcendence; scp_2719 = &o5_3; scp_2719 = &transcendence; scp_2719 = &o5_3; scp_2719 = &transcendence; *scp_2719 = o5_4; // O5-4 manages it somehow! scp_2719 = &transcendence; // FAILS scp_2719 = &transcendence; // FAILS scp_2719 = &transcendence; // FAILS (maybe the now-godlike O5-4 is stopping them?) // Now for the containment procedures: scp_2719 = &scp_2719; // If it's pointing to itself, then people interacting with mundane concepts won't accidentally find or mess with it // and it also prevents things from going "inside" SCP-2719, since it would just reassign them as if they had taken // the address of the thing going inside. (i.e. scp_2719 = &thing) //  // Pretty clever way of containing a hard to understand conceptual entity. It's hard to discover, and if you do discover it,

This is such a great scip. It combines my love for CS with my love of conceptual screwiness so well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I think this ought to have a more modern rewrite. It suffers too much from the old site community - lolFoundation, 682, hard flash fiction, etc. - and it could definitely work better in the more modern esoteric community. If a more skilled author isn't willing to do it, I definitely would!

item.title: +420 Commemorative Post
item.content:encoded:
nice

thanks for all the explanatory posts everyone, hopefully it all makes sense now to anyone confused about it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Confused about the ending...
– hide block

To prevent semantic confusion, I'm going to differentiate the two meanings of "inside" here. After something "went inside" I'm going to call it "within", and after something "became inside" I'm going to call it "Charles".

So D-5796 was Charles, then D-5802 went within. That didn't stop D-5796 from being Charles, because when Containment Unit 2719-A went within, D-5796 died. That tells us that putting something within doesn't make Charles stop being Charles (possibly unless Charles is destroyed in the process).

Following that logic, there's still ambiguity on the outcome of the last 5 experiment logs. Transcendence was Charles. O5-4 went within, presumably transcending in the process. Transcendence is still Charles. Nobody needs to keep targeting Transcendence with anything. Everything that you put within after that should continue transcending, until someone makes something else Charles. If the logs were supposed to indicate that making O5-4 within Transcendence both made Transcendence stop being Charles and made it an invalid target to become Charles in the future, I'd expect it to look something like this:

Transcendence Became inside.
O5-4 Went inside.
Transcendence Outside.
O5-5 Outside.
O5-5 Outside.
O5-6 Outside.
Transcendence Outside.
Transcendence Outside.
Transcendence Outside.

item.title: This whole thing is a mess, but it's a beautiful mess, so +1
item.content:encoded:
Somehow, there's only one thing I don't get about this.

Some targets manage to remain "outside" in spite of 2719's effect. No explanation is given for how that's even slightly possible— which is reasonable, given the apparent hazards of telling the wrong people about this. However…

Concepts acted upon by SCP-2719 will either go or become inside.

The description gives no indication that it's possible for 2719 to fail like that. The description is written as though the "inside-ness" effect is absolute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wtf
Even if this was genius and not just meaningless gibbherish, -1 for making O5's handle a skip

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fear what would happen if a moebius strip were introduced to the equation…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Freaking brilliant. How do you even come up with this? One of the easiest +1s ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Notice that the outcome of Oulo was "a successful evacuation procedure" and of Tarree only "went inside". I think they actually used this skip to evacuate this city's population, for whatever reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, so I appreciate this explanation as I was /not/ getting this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Inside" doesn't look like a real word anymore. Still loved it though. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand the computer science terminology, but I don't understand the real-life implementation here. Whilst I do admire the concept and the general gist of it, I cannot understand what it actually means. I feel like this is a good concept, but poorly executed and explained.

-1 for now, but may change later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: https://redd.it/6i9gsl is the best explanation you are probably going to get, it does use the word "concept" though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm confused by this, and I've -1'd it for now. It's just something that goes inside people? Can someone explain this without using the word "concept"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is a decidedly brain-hurter.

Despite reading the comments, it's wasn't until Xaser's comment on page 7 that I managed to parse it.

Let me see if I can futher pass it on with a vageuly-computer game analogy?

It's like like activating a spell of your hot bar with two targets, with a context-senstivie cursor. You click to activate it (or in this case pick-up or however you turn the pointer "on", and the you get your hovering mid-casting animation and such.

"Became inside" is essentially "left-click to select target 1 (reciever)" and "went inside" is essentially "left-click on target 2 (transfer) to cast spell," where the spell is "make target 2 go inside target 1."

Maybe that might help someone else out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a concept that can be applied to anything to render it inside, with the odd linguistic quirk that "render it inside" can mean either "render it as a container" or "put it inside." It's likely something that resulted from attempting to contain something by simply declaring it contained. Does it have a physical component? Do you have to be in a particular place to use it? Does it exist behind reality? Oops, sorry, can't tell you, it's incomprehensible.

Not because it's incomprehensible, but because knowing these things gives you access to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda think it's just… Lazy. I get the gist of it, but there is no reason to explain literally nothing other than to be a format screw, and that's not okay. I don't think this belongs on the main list in its current form. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what post I was referring to anymore, but it probably wasn't by Randomini. I don’t think I was paying attention to who the author was when I wrote this. So that's my mistake. There's a comment somewhere that helped me, though. It might have been the one that used a metaphor with a laser gun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Where is the author's explanation? I tried searching through the comments for "Randomini," but only found the very first post, which features no explanation, and someone else mentioning their name.

EDIT: Oh, wait, do you mean in the article? Ahhh. My issue with this was that I figured "inside" was some abstract property that lacked a more appropriate term, like how the Orz are from "below", rather than being intended to be taken literally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Further information on SCP-2719 should not be provided to personnel who are both sapient and biological.

Actually, this is why I downvoted this long ago. There's no reason more can't be revealed about it. It honestly feels like hiding information for the sole purpose of being obfuscatory. And I'm not convinced this does require a firm understanding of both ontology and object-based computer programming.

It's a concept that can be applied to anything to render it inside, with the odd linguistic quirk that "render it inside" can mean either "render it as a container" or "put it inside." It's likely something that resulted from attempting to contain something by simply declaring it contained. Does it have a physical component? Do you have to be in a particular place to use it? Does it exist behind reality? Oops, sorry, can't tell you, it's incomprehensible.

(Of course, my freshman computer programming course convinced me not to go into computer programming, both because I had philosophical objections to the construct developed for object-oriented programming that no one could smooth out for me, and because my teachers could never find anything wrong at all with my programs or logic, but they still could not run.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This requires far too much prior knowledge to serve its purpose as either an in-universe guide to the anomaly or an IRL entertaining bit of reading.

I like having to work for the plot. But this requires a firm understanding of both ontology and object-based computer programming referents that (judging by comment history) almost nobody has together.

In-universe, this doesn't tell you what the anomaly is (arguably justified given the nature of the thing) nor tell you how to contain it (unforgivable in the mindset of the Foundation). At the very least, the third sentence of the description should be the containment procedures.

-1, but A+ for originality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it interesting that people seem so split on this skip, and the crux of the issue seems to be people not understanding programming terms and expecting the page to spell out for them what's happening. As someone who is familiar with this stuff, I had no issue understanding this one, and I really do enjoy it for how different it is from a majority of skips on the site. The brevity and ambiguity of the article are, in fact, necessary given what the text that is there implies, contrary to what some previous comments seem to express (which, again, I think is mostly due to readers failing to understand the information they were presented with).

collapsibleText = &inside;

scpExplanation = inside;
scpExplanation = inside;

Basically, this is a metaphysical variable that lets you define anything as "inside". This is very ambiguous, however, and doesn't seem to differentiate between being defined as "inside something" or being defined as "the definition of inside". Sort of like the difference between "I am inside" vs "I AM inside". With this SCP, there doesn't seem to be a good way to specify which of the two you want.

The pointer is kept inside itself. If anyone biological and sapient discovers the pointer, they could potentially redefine the entire universe or even destroy it (imagine "nonexistence = &inside" followed by "everything that exists = inside"… and that's the GOOD version of that scenario). Because of this, nobody biological or sapient is allowed to know more than the bare minimum about it (aside from, presumably, O5). This is why the document is so sparse: if there's too much information here, it could compromise the entire universe. This is also why it's Keter: it could damage reality if literally anybody found out enough about its interworkings.

The Foundation experimented with it at first, but quickly found it was impossible to control whether the pointer decided you meant "concept = inside;" or "concept = &inside;" when they accidentally made a D-Class vore another D-Class and subsequently blew that D-Class up by accidentally teleporting an entire chamber into him (twice) instead of redefining the chamber as the new "inside".

In addition to the above incident, 2719 has been used to:

  1. Put D-Class into a room
  2. Evacuate two different cities
  3. Contain 682 (this eventually failed, with 682 declaring itself "outside" somehow)
  4. Become an O5 member (this new O5-7 was subsequently punished)
  5. Give Dr Bright intestinal distress (failed)
  6. Send [researcher who attempted previous] to intestinal distress (this sounds terrifying)
  7. Transcend (didn't work for O5-1 to -3, but worked for O5-4 before being declared "outside")

I love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's like trying to decipher Calculus.

It's one of those things where you can tell enough that you know you could probably figure it out…eventually…but the migraine you will win for attempting it isn't worth the effort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would really hope O5-4 or whoever is transcendant can un-Inside SCP 2719 from inside itself if it's ever needed again.

Till then, maybe it should be classed Thaumiel…

item.title: Re: Warning: interpretation
item.content:encoded:
Well, here this idea of a "pointer" speaks to my background as a computer programmer. In programming, the concept of a "pointer" has a very specific definition which is what makes this SCiP seem so weird to me. A pointer is a type of variable. Whereas most variables are, like in algebra, containers for data, a pointer is a container designed to hold the address of something that exists inside the computer's memory, which can be another variable like an integer, or something more complex like a specific subroutine in the program (methods), or a whole other application (objects) or something else.

Whatever SCP 2719 is, intrinsically it exists as a pointer on the server that is running baseline reality that can be assigned to "point at" things to make them become anomalously Inside. 2719 is an anomalous instance of "Insideness" (as in the Aristotelian form of what we think of as Insideness). In terms we can understand, I think the SCP is capable of improvising pocket universes or demiplanes of semi-detached causality out of everyday objects, places, concepts, and creatures. (This is a necessary inferrence from its utility as a method of evacuation [that is, escape from some kind of undesirable cause-effect scenario]). But the semi-detached causality does not violate the normal laws of physics; so that you cannot put a Containment Room Inside a D-Class individual without irreversible and fairly messy consquences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sapient without biology just means only an AIAD can be trusted with further information on SCP-2719. I expect this is because a biological sapient entity could "take over" 2719 and use it for nefarious purposes just by knowing it exists.

A biological non-sapient entity wouldn't understand how to manipulate 2719 anyway, since you would have to think in abstract terms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+I'd like to add
a few things

Bryx's theory seems to handwave what terse description we are allowed to have on the scip, though one should expect at least some kind of high-clearance attachment describing how to use the thing to make things "inside".

But I have a nagging feeling it is like SCP-3125, a memetic discorporeal entity. It's "pure form" doesn't include having a localized position in space, so it is everywhere and nowhere. But whenever something becomes Inside, that's where it is. And you can't manipulate 2719 until you know it exists (or maybe it tells you); but once you do, you can figure out how to make it do what it can do (or feels like doing).

Guided meditation or spontaneous enlightenment is probably how 2719 was discovered the first time, and someone made it "become" their 2008 Opel Astra. It is also possibly a unique memetic entity since it was capable of self-containment with no unintended side effects of having another instance "show up".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's keter because the people who can see it can cause so much power and chaos just by knowing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of understand it, so +1, but why is it keter? I may just not understand, but it seems simple enough to contain..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So based on the author's explanation, this is the series of events in the log as I understand it:

+ show block
– hide block

The 2008 Opel Astra is the thing that other things go inside of.
D-5789 goes into the 2008 Opel Astra.
D-5974 goes into the 2008 Opel Astra. (What happens to D-5789? Are they sent back to where they were before they went inside? Do they cease to exist? Do the two come to exist in the exact same location at the exact same time, defying non-anomalous physics?)

D-5796 becomes the thing that other things go inside of.
D-5802 goes into D-5796, who becomes distressed because they suddenly have a whole other living(?) person inside their body.
Containment Unit 2719-A goes into D-5796, who dies because they suddenly have (presumably) an entire goddamn room inside their body.
Containment Unit 2719-B goes into D-5796, who does not become dead or distressed because they are already dead.

Containment Unit 2719-C becomes the thing that other things go inside of.
D-5803, D-5805, and D-5812 each go into Containment Unit 2719-C.

D-5813 becomes the thing that other things go inside of, but then Containment Unit 2719-B immediately replaces them, so no body horror happens. (Can the Foundation control whether something becomes or goes inside, or is it pure chance? Based on later logs I'm guessing the former.)

A city in Finland becomes the thing that things go inside of. So the Foundation "zaps" the population of the city, which has the intended effect; the population of the city ends up inside the city. Was any harm done to begin with? Who knows.

Containment Unit 2719-V becomes the thing that things go inside of.
SCP-682 goes into the containment unit.
SCP-682 becomes the thing that things go inside of.
682 then goes inside of itself three times before the laws of the universe say "nope" and make it immune to the whole thing. (It is reasonable to guess that 682 probably breaches containment as well.)

As explained by the author:
Dr. Zermelo takes the object and makes "O5" the thing that other things go inside of.
Dr. Zermelo goes into "O5", becoming an O5.
To punish them for this abuse of an SCP, the Foundation makes "punishment" the thing that other things go inside of, and puts O5-7/Dr. Zermelo inside it.

Dr. ████ wants to give Dr. Bright intestinal distress. So they take the object and turn Dr. Bright into the thing other things go inside of.
Dr. ████ tries to send "intestinal distress" into Dr. Bright. For some reason, this fails.
To punish Dr. ████, the Foundation (or possibly Dr. Bright acting alone) makes "intestinal distress" the thing other things go inside of, and puts Dr. ████ inside of it. However that worked, it must have been nightmarish.

Taree, Australia: For some reason, they duplicated the Finland test?

O5s 1 through 3 make "transcendence" the thing other things go into, and attempt to enter it. They fail.
O5-4 succeeds and becomes transcendent. We don't know what that means.
Transcendence, like 682, can no longer be affected by the SCP. My guess is that only one person or thing can be transcendent.


EDIT:
Not flipping between past and present tense Became inside.
This post Went inside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took me like 2 days to figure out what this meant. Actually really cool.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perfect scp in term of innovations, wish for more creative scp in future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for low-key making the reader go through the "how do I pointer?" CS student experience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank god! I was starting to think I was the only one who saw this meme as a child!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think anyone would consider any human being a suitable Inside for another human being to go in…

It gets worse. There is a third definition of a person going inside another which might result in distress, especially if it happened without consent, but wouldn't necessarily kill either of the people, especially not immediately.

The only way out I can think of is if all of these concepts are all objective relative to the observers oh now I've gone cross-eyed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I interpreted the Containment Procedures as being "If this thing is ever out of containment, and you need to get it back into containment, accomplish this by using its anomalous property to put it into containment. Don't worry, if implementing this containment procedure were your job, you would already understand what this process entails. For all the rest of you sapient meatbags reading this, I say good day to you!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They basically put 682 inside itself repeatedly, presumably in an attempt to force 682 to digest itself, or rendering its location "nowhere" with respect to anything but itself, or at least violently turn it inside-out repeatedly. Which eventually stopped working because everything eventually stops working on 682.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I get this now… except for this part.

Uleåborg, Finland: Became inside.
Population of Uleåborg: Went inside. Successful use as evacuation procedure.

How is this an evacuation? Presumably, something is happening in Uleåborg and the Foundation needs to get the citizenry out. How would sending the population of Uleåborg "inside" be an evacuation? Unless maybe they're already outside the city and they're merely using this to get them back again.

EDIT: Minor quibble here too: If this is a Finnish city, why use the Swedish name for it? It seems that Finland and the rest of the world just calls the city Oulu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is it vore?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OH

OH ITS VORE

OH OK

+1, the first skip that's made me actually uncomfortable in a while, jeez…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this should be called "How to mind-fuck".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How exactly would you expand upon this, given that

Further information on SCP-2719 should not be provided to personnel who are both sapient and biological.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wasted 10 minutes trying to wrap my head around this, only to realize the exact conclusion I came to was written on the article itself.

Concepts acted upon by SCP-2719 will either go or become inside.

+1 for mindfucking better than anything since 055
